content:
Anatomy of the AI "Hijacking the Gartner Hype Cycle"

CEO marketing speech: Overpromise, then before reach the underdeliver point, we got a new 'magic' model release that does the same thing faster, restarting the overpromise-and-underdeliver loop.

There is a reason:

This is called the Gartner's Hype Cycle. It describes the macro wave a new technology takes, from an "Innovation Trigger" to the "Peak of Inflated Expectations", crashing into the "Trough of Disillusionment", before reaching the "Plateau of Productivity."

https://www.forbes.com/sites/johnwerner/2024/07/18/the-trough-of-disillusionment-and-four-outliers-on-the-gartner-hype-cycle/

[Property of Forbes.com]

Gartner Stage
Market Reality
The CEO Marketing Playbook

1. Innovation Trigger
A tech breakthrough happens in the lab.

The Keynote Reveal: Demoing cherry-picked capabilities and teasing a game-changing revolution.

2. Peak of Inflated Expectations
Wild media speculation and unrealistic hype.

The Overpromise: Promising an "AI co-pilot for everything" to drive up valuation and capture market share.

3. Trough of Disillusionment
Users realize the tech is flawed or limited.

The Underdelivery: The public hits compute limits, high prices, hallucinations, or missing features.

4. Slope of Enlightenment
2nd & 3rd generation iterations stabilize the tech.

The "Turbo" Pivot: "Introducing Model v1.5 Flash! It's 5x faster and 80% cheaper!" (Selling efficiency as breakthrough innovation).

5. Plateau of Productivity
The tech becomes a stable, mature utility.

The Shortcut / Cycle Reset: Companies stop here. Instead of letting the model become a mature commodity, the CEO launches Model 2.0 to trigger a brand-new cycle.

[ 1. Visionary Overpromise ] ──> [ 2. Underdelivery Reality ]
▲ │
│ ▼
[ 4. Repeat with Next Gen ] <── [ 3. "Turbo/Flash" Pivot ]

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Why the Loop Never Ends

In traditional technology, a product eventually reaches the Plateau of Productivity where it becomes stable, reliable, and relatively boring (like email, databases, or cloud storage).

In modern tech marketing, companies artificially disrupt their own products right at Stage 4. By packaging a faster, cheaper version of the same product as a new "magic model," they force a cycle reset, hijacking the loop before reaching true product stability.

CEO Visionary Overpromise

This is both the ignition switch and the reset button of the Hype Loop. Without it, technology would follow a straight, healthy line of progress. With it, progress turns into a hamster wheel.

The Visionary Overpromise is what keeps the loop spinning. It distracts users from today's broken reality by constantly selling them tomorrow's sci-fi fantasy.

┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
▼ │
┌────────────────────────────────────┐ ┌─────────────┴──────────────┐
│ Visionary Overpromise │ │ The "Magic" Pivot │
│ (Ignition Switch & Reset Button) │ │ (Faster / Cheaper / v2.0) │
└─────────────────┬──────────────────┘ └─────────────▲──────────────┘
│ │
▼ │
┌────────────────────────────────────┐ ┌─────────────┴──────────────┐
│ Underdelivery / MVP │──────►│ Trough of Disillusionment │
└────────────────────────────────────┘ └────────────────────────────┘

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Why CEOs Keep Claiming "This Will Replace All Developers?

Strategic distraction:

Because the rhetoric around replacing software engineers is a strategic distraction and a marketing strategy. By constantly declaring a futuristic paradigm shift, leadership forces the product narrative back to Stage 1, bypassing accountability for current reliability and performance issues.

Why Do CEOs Overpromise?

•
Targeting the CFO, Not the Engineer: Enterprise sales target non-technical executives focused on cutting payroll, not developers looking for reliable workflows.

•
Competing for Budget: When pitching a software tool, companies compete for small software budgets. When pitching labor replacement, they compete for massive corporate payroll budgets.

•
Prestige Marketing: Claiming a product is powerful enough to displace millions of skilled workers tricks the market into believing the underlying tech is more capable than it actually is.

•
Regulatory Moats: Framed as "existentially powerful," tech giants can lobby governments for strict safety rules. These expensive compliance hurdles lock out open-source competitors, securing an incumbent monopoly.

How to Break the Hype Loop

You can't stop CEO keynotes, they want to sell you a vision, a revolutionary paradigm shift, but must protect your Architecture and budget and separate the magic from reality. Here is how engineering teams break the cycle:

•
Decouple Your Architecture: Never lock your codebase into a single vendor's API. Build model-agnostic abstraction layers so you can swap providers with a single line of code when quality drops or prices rise.

•
Kill the Public Benchmarks: Ignore keynote leaderboards. Evaluate new models exclusively against your own closed repository, production logs, and real edge cases.

•
Measure the "Verification Tax": Fast code generation means nothing if senior engineers spend hours debugging subtle hallucinations. Calculate ROI by total delivery time, not raw generation speed.

•
Leverage Open Weights: Self-hosted, open-source models strip away marketing spin, turning AI from an expensive subscription treadmill into a cheap, predictable utility.

Bottom line: Stop buying tomorrow's roadmap. Separe the Visionary Overpromise from reality. Demand contractual SLAs today, run your own evals, and treat every model as an unreliable utility until proven otherwise.
